plainMeaning: I joyfully sing the names of more of the Jinas who are alive and teaching right now in the great central land of Mahāvideha — Viśāla, Vajradhara, Candrānana, Candrabāhu, Bhujaṅga, Īśvara and Nemiprabha — and I fix my mind on them.
recitation: Viśāla Vajaṃdhara sāhu, Candrānana Candrabāhu; Bhujaṃga Īśvara gāuṃ, Nemiprabhu citta e lāuṃ.

wordByWord.gloss: Viśāla(dhara) — a living (viharamāṇa) Jina of Mahāvideha
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit viśāla 'vast, broad'; here the proper name of one of the twenty currently-living Tīrthaṅkaras (also given as Viśāladhara).
wordByWord.notes: Tenth in the standard list of twenty viharamāṇa Jinas; the previous verse named numbers five through nine.

wordByWord.gloss: Vajradhara — a living Jina of Mahāvideha
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit vajra-dhara 'bearer of the thunderbolt/diamond'; vernacular vajaṃdhara for vajradhara, with anusvāra for the conjunct -jr-.
Prakrit/Apabhraṃśa sāhu (< Sanskrit sādhu) 'good, holy, saintly'; here a reverential appellation applied to the Jinas just named, chosen to chime with -bāhu.
wordByWord.notes: Not a separate Jina-name; an honorific. Some reciters hear it adverbially ('well, fittingly [I praise]'); the honorific reading suits the roll-call.

wordByWord.gloss: Candrānana — a living Jina of Mahāvideha
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit candra-ānana 'moon-faced'; sandhi candra + ānana = candrānana.
wordByWord.notes: Twelfth viharamāṇa Jina. The same name also belongs to one of the four eternal (śāśvata) Jina-names invoked elsewhere in the hymn; here it denotes the living Mahāvideha Jina.

wordByWord.gloss: Candrabāhu — a living Jina of Mahāvideha
wordByWord.notes: Thirteenth viharamāṇa Jina; rhymes with sāhu, closing the first line.

wordByWord.gloss: Bhujaṅga(ma) — a living Jina of Mahāvideha
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit bhujaṃga(ma) 'serpent' (lit. 'going by coils'); the proper name of the fourteenth viharamāṇa Jina, often given as Bhujaṅgama.

wordByWord.gloss: Īśvara — a living Jina of Mahāvideha
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit īśvara 'lord, sovereign'; here a proper Jina-name, not the theistic deity.
Gujarati gāuṁ, first-person singular present of gāvuṁ 'to sing' (< Sanskrit root gai-, gāyati).
wordByWord.notes: Governs the names of the line: 'Bhujaṅga, Īśvara — I sing [them].' Marks the verse's mode as vocal praise (stuti).

wordByWord.gloss: Lord Nemiprabha — a living Jina of Mahāvideha
wordByWord.etymology: Nemiprabha (Sanskrit nemi-prabhā 'radiance of the wheel-rim') + the honorific prabhu 'Lord', vernacularly fused as nemiprabhu.
wordByWord.notes: Sixteenth viharamāṇa Jina. Distinct from Tīrthaṅkara Neminātha of our Bharata caubīsī.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit/Prakrit citta 'mind, attention, consciousness' (< root cit- 'to perceive').
wordByWord.notes: Object of lāuṁ; 'I fix this mind [on him].'

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati demonstrative e 'this, that, these'; here pointing to the Jinas just named.
wordByWord.notes: Can be read with citta ('this mind') or with the Jinas ('on these I fix [my] mind'); the sense is the same act of focusing.

wordByWord.gloss: I fix / I apply / I attach
wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati lāuṁ, first-person singular of lāvavuṁ / lagāḍvuṁ 'to apply, attach, fix' (< Sanskrit root lag- 'to adhere').
wordByWord.notes: 'citta e lāuṁ' = 'I fix [my] mind on these.' The verse turns from gāuṁ (singing aloud) to citta lāuṁ (inward fixing of the mind).

literalTranslation: Viśāla, Vajradhara — the holy ones — Candrānana, Candrabāhu; Bhujaṅga, Īśvara I sing; on Nemiprabhu this mind I fix.
idiomaticTranslation: Viśāla and Vajradhara, those holy ones; Candrānana and Candrabāhu; Bhujaṅga and Īśvara — I sing them all aloud; and on Lord Nemiprabha I now fasten my very mind.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This verse carries forward the roll-call of the viharamāṇa Jinas — the Tīrthaṅkaras living and preaching at this moment in Mahāvideha — that the preceding verse began with Sujāta, Svayamprabha, Ṛṣabhānana, Anantavīrya and Sūraprabha. Here the poet names the next seven of the canonical twenty: Viśāla(dhara), Vajradhara, Candrānana, Candrabāhu, Bhujaṅga(ma), Īśvara and Nemiprabha; the following verse will close the set with Vīrasena, Mahābhadra, Devayaśas and Ajitavīrya. The single word sāhu ('holy ones') is not another name but a reverential appellation that gathers the named Jinas under one breath of devotion while neatly rhyming with Candrabāhu. The verse's real movement, though, lies in its two verbs. The first line and a half are governed by gāuṁ, 'I sing' — outward, vocal praise. The closing words shift register: citta e lāuṁ, 'I fix my mind on these.' Devotion rises from the tongue to the mind, from naming to dwelling. Naming Nemiprabhu last and then immediately 'fixing the mind' makes the whole list culminate not in more sound but in inward attention. Note too that Candrānana here is the living Mahāvideha Jina; the identical name recurs among the four eternal Jina-names elsewhere in the hymn, a small reminder that these auspicious names echo across ages and worlds.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on the Jain teaching of the viharamāṇa Jinas: although our region of Bharata is now in the descending fifth ara, where no Tīrthaṅkara is born, twenty fully awakened Jinas are always present and teaching in the five Mahāvideha realms, where conditions like the fourth ara persist perpetually. To name and sing them is therefore to affirm that the tīrtha — a living ford to liberation — is never wholly absent from the universe, even when it is absent from one's own land. The closing turn from gāuṁ to citta lāuṁ enacts the ascent from dravya/vacana devotion (vocal naming) toward bhāva-pūjā (heartfelt, mental veneration): the merit of the act lies finally in where the mind rests, not merely in what the mouth utters. Steadying the mind on living Jinas in this way is, in the tradition, a direct nourishment of samyaktva (right faith).
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited at dawn, this verse invites the worshipper to let the seven names run like beads of a mālā and then deliberately stop singing and rest the attention on them — practising, in a few seconds, the very shift from speech to inward focus that the words describe. It is a reminder that even today one can mentally bow to a Jina who is alive and teaching right now, so devotion need never feel orphaned.

crossReferences.text: Śrīmaṇḍara-stavana — hymn to Sīmandhara as foremost of the twenty viharamāṇa Tīrthaṅkaras
crossReferences.source: Ṛṣi Rāmakṛṣṇa (Tessitori Collection, Biblioteca Civica 'V. Joppi', Udine)

crossReferences.text: Standard enumeration of the twenty viharamāṇa Jinas of the five Mahāvideha realms

sources.title: Simandhar (Simandhara) — living Tīrthaṅkara of Mahāvideh Kshetra
sources.consultedFor: Confirming that twenty Tīrthaṅkaras (four in each of the five Mahāvideha realms) are currently living and preaching — the viharamāṇa Jinas of whom this verse names several.

sources.title: Viharamāna (विहरमान) — definitions
sources.consultedFor: Corroborating viharamāṇa = the 'twenty living Tīrthaṅkaras' of Mahāvideha, and the Śrīmaṇḍara-stavana cross-reference.

sources.title: Tirthankara
sources.consultedFor: The jina/tīrthaṅkara concept and the cosmological framing (avasarpiṇī fifth ara of Bharata vs. perpetual presence in Mahāvideha) underlying the verse's devotion.

notes: The verse names viharamāṇa Jinas #10–16 of the standard twenty (Viśāla/Viśāladhara, Vajradhara, Candrānana, Candrabāhu, Bhujaṅga/Bhujaṅgama, Īśvara, Nemiprabha); this matches the surrounding verses, which name #5–9 (preceding) and #17–20 (following). 'સાહુ (sāhu)' is read as the honorific sādhu ('holy one'), not a Jina-name; an adverbial reading ('well/fittingly') is possible. 'વજંધર' is the vernacular form of Vajradhara (anusvāra for the -jr- conjunct). 'ચંદ્રાનન' here is the living Mahāvideha Jina, coinciding in name with one of the four eternal (śāśvata) Jina-names used elsewhere in the hymn. The daṇḍa (।) and book punctuation are preserved as printed. Exact spellings/order of the twenty viharamāṇa names vary slightly across editions; readings here follow the hymn's own sequence and the Anchalgaccha (Vidhipaksha) edition's orthography.
